serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of roasted chicken and dang gui roasted duck rice served at a Singapore hawker centre typically weighs approximately 350g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice topped with slices of roasted chicken and dang gui-marinated roasted duck, accompanied by a small amount of gravy and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Chicken & Dang Gui Roasted Duck Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Roasted Chicken & Dang Gui Roasted Duck Rice' (cr_id: G00010700), confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from the HPB fat value using standard poultry roasted dish fatty acid ratios (approximately 33% of total fat as saturated fat), as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (jasmine-type), which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The dish is approximately 55–60% rice by weight, making white rice the primary glycaemic driver. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 88.2g carbohydrate in 350g serving) / 100 ≈ 63.5, rounded to 63.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g; gravy and soy-based marinades are the primary sodium contributors
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced gravy, remove poultry skin to lower saturated fat, and monitor cumulative sodium intake if consuming additional condiments or sauces alongside this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI white rice base (GI ~72) contributes 25.2g carbohydrate per 100g, producing a high glycaemic load of approximately 63 per standard 350g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce portion size significantly, add non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption, and remove poultry skin to reduce saturated fat that may worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 1.39g/100g from roasted poultry skin and drippings; sodium at 231 mg/100g from soy-based marinade and gravy; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g in HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should remove skin from both chicken and duck to reduce saturated fat intake, request minimal gravy to limit sodium, and consider reducing portion size to manage overall energy and fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dang gui (Angelica sinensis) provides anti-inflammatory properties; both meats are poultry (not red meat); saturated fat is low at approximately 1.39g/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume this dish in moderation; removing poultry skin reduces saturated fat, and requesting less gravy keeps sodium within RA-safe limits, while the dang gui herb component may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Chicken and Dang Gui Roasted Duck Rice (烧鸡当归烤鸭饭) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of steamed white rice served with slices of both Cantonese-style roasted chicken and dang gui (当归, Angelica sinensis) marinated roasted duck. The dish is a staple at Chinese roasted meat (烧腊, siu laap) stalls found throughout Singapore's hawker centres and food courts. The roasted chicken is typically prepared by marinating the bird in a blend of soy sauce, five-spice powder, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil before roasting at high temperature to achieve a lacquered, caramelised skin with juicy, tender meat. The dang gui roasted duck is distinguished by the inclusion of dang gui root — a traditional Chinese medicinal herb with a mildly bitter, earthy, and slightly sweet flavour — in the marinade and braising liquid, imparting a distinctive herbal aroma and reputed tonic properties. The rice is steamed plain or lightly seasoned and serves as the carbohydrate base. The dish is typically finished with a drizzle of savoury roasting gravy and may be accompanied by sliced cucumber and a small portion of dark soy sauce. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate, with notable dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g and potassium at 327 mg/100g. The high GI white rice base (GI ~72) makes this dish a high glycaemic load meal, requiring caution for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium at 231 mg/100g is moderate and primarily derived from soy-based marinades and gravy. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to the use of pork-derived ingredients in some preparations, and contains soy and sesame allergens. It is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner and is a culturally significant comfort food in Singapore's Chinese culinary heritage.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010700) with a confidence of 0.95, representing an exact match to this specific dish. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes under Project Veritas v2. Saturated fat is estimated from total fat using standard poultry roasted dish fatty acid ratios and is documented accordingly.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.39g/100g) is estimated as approximately 33% of total fat (4.21g/100g) based on standard fatty acid profiles for mixed roasted poultry dishes; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean roasted poultry rice dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100g in the HPB source; this is an unusual value for a dish containing roasted poultry and may reflect a reporting limitation or analytical method in the HPB database — confirmed zero per HPB source. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the dominant white rice carbohydrate component (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). 5. Halal status is marked false as traditional Cantonese siu laap preparations may include pork-derived ingredients; consumers should verify with individual stall operators. 6. FoodEx2 code A0FJP and FoodOn URI FOODON_00002473 are used as approximate ontology anchors; no exact codes exist for this specific dish.
